_note: Three IntakeForm objects for showcase mode (spec §6). Run each through scripts/pregenerate.ts once and commit the results to data/showcase/. 'focused' should fire ~0-1 checks, 'fuzzy' fires vague_audience + vanity_metric + no_target + no_evidence + no_assumption, 'kitchen_sink' fires kitchen_sink. Keys starting with _ are ignored by the pregenerate script.

focused.feature_name: Reply templates for the seller inbox
focused.one_liner: Reusable reply templates with auto-fill variables, inside the seller inbox.
focused.target_user: Solo Etsy-style shop owners handling 20+ buyer conversations a day
focused.problem: Sellers answer the same five questions — shipping time, sizing, customization, returns, delays — dozens of times a week. Today they copy-paste from a Notes app or retype from memory: slow, error-prone, and inconsistent answers show up in negative reviews.
focused.evidence: 12 seller interviews in June; 8 of 12 unprompted showed me a personal notes doc of canned answers. Also the top tooling complaint in two seller-forum threads I pulled.
focused.proposed_solution: A templates drawer inside the inbox: save any sent reply as a template, insert one with two clicks, and variables auto-fill buyer name and order number. Ships with five starter templates.

success_metric.metric: Share of replies sent via template among sellers active in week 2

focused.riskiest_assumption: Buyers won't read templated replies as impersonal in a marketplace built on 'handmade.'

fuzzy.one_liner: An AI coach that builds personalized workout plans for everyone.
fuzzy.target_user: Everyone who wants to get in shape
fuzzy.problem: People download fitness apps but quit after a couple of weeks because they lose motivation. Existing apps are generic and don't keep users engaged over the long term.
fuzzy.proposed_solution: An AI that generates a personalized workout plan and automatically adjusts it based on your progress, goals, and available equipment.

kitchen_sink.one_liner: One app for everything students need on campus.
kitchen_sink.target_user: Commuter students at large public universities
kitchen_sink.problem: Commuter students miss out on campus life. Events are scattered across email, flyers, and five different apps; buying used textbooks means sketchy Facebook groups; and finding study partners is nearly impossible when you leave campus at 3pm.
kitchen_sink.evidence: Informal survey of 40 students from my old commuter group chat; 31 said they usually hear about events after they've happened.
kitchen_sink.proposed_solution: A campus super-app: a personalized events feed, a used textbook and furniture marketplace, study-group matching based on course schedules, campus food pickup and delivery, and an anonymous forum for questions and confessions.

success_metric.metric: Weekly active students at the pilot campus

kitchen_sink.riskiest_assumption: Students will switch from the single-purpose apps they already use to an all-in-one.
